metadata.title: The 2018 Mw 7.5 Palu 'supershear' earthquake ruptures geological fault's multisegment separated by large bends: results from integrating field measurements, LiDAR, swath bathymetry and seismic-reflection data
metadata.study_area: Palu region, Central Sulawesi — onshore and offshore Palu Bay and northern PKF
metadata.method: Field surveys (Oct 2018, Jan 2020), LiDAR (1-m grid, BIG), swath bathymetry (Baruna Jaya/NUS-INHO, 5-m grid), single-channel seismic reflection (JICA sparker)
conclusions: 2018 Palu earthquake ruptured 177 km total along the mature, pre-existing geological Palu-Koro Fault — NOT a hidden immature fault (contradicting previous InSAR/remote-sensing interpretations)
PKF divided into 4 segments separated by large bends: Tanimbaya (N, compressional), Donggala (submarine, transition), Palu (pull-apart, extensional), Saluki (S)
Offshore rupture: ~110 km submarine, from Palu Bay northward past Tanimbaya Peninsula toward Sulawesi Trench
Inland rupture: ~67 km NNW-SSE to N-S; left-lateral slip up to 6 m near Palu beach, with 5-10% dip-slip component on average
Maximum vertical displacement ~5 m at the 12 km NW-SE releasing fault bend (Skm33-45)
Two large releasing bends form pull-apart structure: 3.5 km wide submarine (Donggala-Palu junction) + 7 km wide inland (southern Palu basin edge)
Long submarine rupture likely triggered massive underwater landslides, contributing significantly to Palu tsunami (inconsistently modelled in studies using only the neck-area fault)
Straight fault geometry allows possibility of early and persistent supershear rupture propagation

properties.name: 2018 Palu Mw 7.5 Mainshock
properties.epicenter_note: Relocated from Supendi et al. (2020); ~15 km NE of a bend in Donggala segment; approximate in this GeoJSON

properties.name: Palu-Koro Fault — Tanimbaya Segment
properties.orientation: N-S curving to NNW-SSE northward toward Sulawesi Trench
properties.geometry_note: Northern segment; fault regionally curves clockwise from Tanimbaya Peninsula toward North Sulawesi Trench. Connects strike-slip to subduction. Separated from Donggala by 4.2 km wide restraining stepover at Nkm105.

properties.name: Palu-Koro Fault — Donggala Segment
properties.tectonic_regime: transitional compressional-extensional; slight restraining bend in middle
properties.geometry_note: Primarily submarine segment (~110 km total offshore rupture). Separated from Tanimbaya by 4.2 km restraining stepover (N) and from Palu segment by 3.5 km releasing bend underwater (Nkm15-20 inside Palu Bay). Main shock nucleated here (~Nkm85 area, ~15 km NE of central restraining bend). Narrow submarine graben valley up to 2700 m wide at ~1000 m depth (Nkm70-90).

properties.name: Palu-Koro Fault — Palu Segment
properties.geometry_note: From Palu Bay mouth (Nkm0/Skm0) southward ~34 km inland to southern basin edge. Left-lateral slip 4-6 m near beach, decreasing southward. Accompanied by dip-slip (east-down) up to 1 m along eastern margin normal faults. Fault narrows from wide offshore graben to sharp inland fault line at beachfront.

properties.name: Palu-Koro Fault — Saluki Segment
properties.geometry_note: Southern segment from the large 7 km releasing bend (Skm33-45) to termination at Skm67 where a 1.5 km wide restraining stepover ends the rupture. Left-lateral slip increases from ~2 m at Skm37 to peak ~3.2 m at Skm49, then decreases to ~1 m at Skm66, terminating at Skm67. The restraining stepover at Skm67 is a segment boundary with the more southerly PKF trace.

properties.name: Palu Pull-Apart Basin
properties.geometry_note: Formed by 7 km wide releasing bend between Palu and Saluki segments. Basin includes Palu city, Palu Bay (Bay = northern submarine part), and elongated valley south of bay. Extensive normal faults along eastern margin accommodating extension. Liquefaction (Balaroa, Sibalaya) and massive landslides contributed to catastrophic effects of 2018 earthquake.
properties.eastern_margin_faults: numerous N-S and NW-SE normal faults (potentially active)
